metadata.title: Agentic Research Course
metadata.author: Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.
metadata.description: Week 6: Grant Proposal Writing -- NOFO to Specific Aims to Self-Review

elements.content:
**Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.**
Assistant Project Scientist, Swartz Center for Computational Neuroscience
UC San Diego

[Open Science Collective](https://osc.earth)

Course: [courses.osc.earth/agentic-research](https://courses.osc.earth/agentic-research/) | Discord: [discord.gg/5dWJCUmUww](https://discord.gg/5dWJCUmUww) | Recording: published to YouTube within 48 h

slides.speakerNotes:
- Welcome back

- Last week: literature review as a managed pipeline with cite-the-card discipline

- Today: the next consumer of that corpus is grant proposals -- Specific Aims is a 1-page literature review

- Format: ~35 min presentation, ~5 min live demo on the practicum, 15 min Q&A

- Course site, Discord, and recording note on the slide

items.text: **Weeks 1-2 -- Git, GitHub, Claude Code.** The safety net and the agent.
**Week 3 -- Project management.** Epic, sub-issues, worktrees, plan mode, `/review-pr`.
**Week 4 -- CI/CD.** Automated checks outside the repo, on every push.
**Week 5 -- Literature review.** Cite-the-card corpus, boomerang reviews.
**Today -- Grants.** A Specific Aims page is a 1-page literature review with deliverables; the Week-5 corpus is the input.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- Each week's structure compounds. Week 6 puts the Week-5 corpus to work.

- The same pipeline pattern -- structure first, cite the card, boomerang on review -- applies to grant writing too.

elements.alt: Three labelled runtimes -- Claude Code, Codex CLI, Copilot in VS Code -- each showing the same seven research-skills plugins installed (manuscript, opencite, scientific-figures, presentation, project, grant, neuroinformatics)
elements.caption: **Same plugins, same prompts, same outputs.** The runtime is now a deployment choice.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Major rewrite landed between Week 5 and Week 6. The skills are now portable across Claude Code, OpenAI's Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot in VS Code (and by extension, Cursor and other VS-Code-derived IDEs).

- The three screenshots are real: Claude Code's /plugin list, Codex CLI's plugin manager, and the @agentPlugins marketplace view inside VS Code. Each shows 7 of 7 research-skills plugins installed.

- The skills + agents protocol is the standard interface; the runtime is just the engine that executes it.

- We will run the rest of today's demo in Claude Code for consistency, but every slash command works equally well in Codex or Copilot.

elements.alt: Before vs after: a Claude-only setup (locked padlock with paid-subscription requirements) becomes an agent-agnostic setup (open padlock with Copilot free tier and Codex CLI free tier)
elements.caption: **GitHub Copilot is free for students.** A first-year PhD can now run `/grant:grant-writing` and `/grant:grant-review` without a paid subscription.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 1x to reveal the callout]

- Land the accessibility point clearly. The course was hard to recommend to LMIC labs and bootstrapped trainees when the only viable runtime was paid Claude Pro plus API credits.

- Now: GitHub Copilot's student tier covers the runtime, GitHub itself is free, and the plugins are open source under permissive licences. Nothing in the pipeline requires a paid subscription for trainees.

- The trade-offs (capability ceiling, latency) differ across runtimes, but the workflow does not.

elements.content: ## Reframe -- Specific Aims = 1-Page Lit Review + Deliverables

items.text: The Week-5 corpus and its synthesis docs feed the Significance section directly.
Cite-the-card discipline transfers verbatim: every Significance and Innovation claim points to a `card.md`.
The Aims add what a lit review does not: **measurable deliverables on a timeline**.
Same boomerang from Week 5: review findings cycle back into Aims or Strategy, not just into prose edits.

elements.content: **Lit review = an argument. Aims = an argument with skin in the game.** The Week-5 pipeline taught you to argue from a corpus; today adds the obligation to put deliverables on the page.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- This is the headline of the talk. Land it cleanly: today's work is not a new thing; it reuses Week 5's machinery.

- 'Skin in the game' = numbered deliverables you are committing to produce within a timeline.

- A Specific Aims page that cannot point to its source paper-cards is just a story; reviewers know.

elements.alt: Five-stage horizontal pipeline (NOFO, Aims, Strategy, Self-review, Figure QA) with a boomerang arrow looping from Self-review back to Aims

elements.content: Order is enforced. Drafting Aims before parsing the NOFO is a **structural error**, not a style choice. Self-review and Figure QA are not optional polish; they are gates.

slides.speakerNotes:
- The hero diagram. Read each stage label aloud.

- NOFO -> Aims -> Strategy -> Self-review -> Figure QA.

- The boomerang arrow is the rigor signal -- a self-review that converges in one shot is hiding what it found.

- Stages 4-5 are the parts trainees most often skip. Today's demo will hit both.

elements.alt: Comparison table of R01, R21, R03, K99/R00, F31/F32, and NSF CAREER mechanisms with audience, aims-page length, strategy length, budget cap, and submission cycle

elements.content: The `grant-writing` skill takes the mechanism as a parameter and pre-populates page budgets, required sections, and review criteria. **Picking the wrong mechanism is the most expensive avoidable error.**

slides.speakerNotes:
- Spend ~30 seconds on each row. The cheat-sheet is a reference, not memorisation.

- For today's practicum: an R21 fits the scope (high-risk pilot, 6-page strategy, $275K total) better than an R01.

- NSF CAREER is on the table for early-career faculty; we focus the rest of the talk on NIH because that is the audience majority.

elements.alt: Five NIH review criteria (Significance, Investigator, Innovation, Approach, Environment) with a 1-9 colour-gradient scoring ladder underneath

elements.content: Each criterion scores **1-9** independently. The integrated impact score is the reviewer's holistic judgement, **not the average**. A 1 in Significance does not save a 5 in Approach.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Five criteria. Memorise them: Significance, Investigator, Innovation, Approach, Environment.

- The integrated impact score (1-9) is the headline; it is what determines funding.

- 'Fundable' band varies by institute and cycle. NIH-wide success rates are ~20%; specific institutes pay out down to impact ~30.

elements.alt: A dense NOFO/FOA page on the left with highlighted lines parses into a structured NOFO.md file on the right capturing mechanism, deadline, page limits, budget cap, required areas, and special requirements

elements.content: The `grant-writing` skill writes `NOFO.md` first. **Every aim, methods choice, and budget line in the rest of the proposal must point back to one of these extracted requirements.**

slides.speakerNotes:
- Step 0 is the cheapest mistake to skip. A misread page limit or eligibility rule wastes months.

- NOFO.md is the contract; everything downstream is checked against it.

- This is the first of three defences (NOFO contract, cite-the-card, boomerang).

elements.alt: Horizontal bar charts for R01, R21, and F31 page budgets showing how Significance, Innovation, Approach, and applicant sections add up to the mechanism's page limit

elements.content: Trim **content**, never **spacing**. Reviewers know 9-pt body and 0.4-in margins on sight. Page over-runs are rejection-before-review.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Allocate before you draft. The skill puts placeholder counts in each section and tracks word/line counts as you write.

- For an R01: ~2p Significance, ~1.5p Innovation, ~8.5p Approach (with ~2p preliminary data, ~5p aim breakdowns, ~1p rigor, ~0.5p timeline).

- For an R21: half of those numbers.

- The page budget is a contract with yourself. Going over by ~10 lines costs ~30 lines of trim later.

elements.content: ## Anatomy of a Specific Aims Page

elements.alt: A 1-page Specific Aims layout with six labelled blocks (Opening, Overarching goal + scope, Aim 1 + hypotheses, Aim 2, optional Aim 3, Expected Impact) and word-count annotations

slides.speakerNotes:
- Six blocks. They earn their space in this order.

- Reviewers read this page first, often only this page on the first pass.

- The bold overarching-goal sentence is the single most important sentence in the proposal; it appears verbatim later in the Strategy too.

items.text: **Parses the NOFO** into `NOFO.md` and **allocates pages** per mechanism, with running counts.
**Drafts Specific Aims** with the 6-block anatomy, style rules pre-applied.
**Drafts Significance / Innovation / Approach** with `cite-the-card` discipline from Week 5.
**Calls** `manuscript:humanizer` for final polish (no AI-vocabulary, no promotional language).

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 6x to reveal fragments]

- The skill is a workflow, not a one-shot draft.

- It runs Step 0 -> Step 6 from the SKILL.md and asks for human checkpoints between steps.

- 'cite-the-card' is the Week-5 rule applied here: every Significance and Innovation claim points to a paper-card by relative path.

- humanizer is the final polish; it strips AI fingerprints (Pattern 1 significance inflation, Pattern 4 promotional language, Pattern 24 hedging) without flattening the prose.

elements.alt: Four style rule cards: bold strategically, italicise hypotheses, active voice with quantification, no em-dashes and define abbreviations on first use

elements.content: These rules read as nit-picky; in study section they are how a senior reviewer **decides whether the writing carries the science**. The skill applies them automatically; you only fight them when you really need to.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Land 'bold strategically' especially. New PIs over-bold; the result is that nothing is bold.

- Hypothesis italic + one sentence + continuation on same line is a Yahya house-style preference, but it is also how NIH expects them. It saves vertical space.

- Active voice ('We will demonstrate') reads as confident; passive ('It will be demonstrated') reads as evasive.

- No em-dashes is partly Yahya's preference, partly a corpus-quality signal: AI overuses em-dashes.

elements.alt: Three vertical columns: Significance (problem, gap, why, success enables), Innovation (conceptual, technical, methodological, field advancement), and Approach (preliminary data, aim-by-aim plan, rigor, timeline)

slides.speakerNotes:
- Three sections, fixed order. The agent will not draft them out of order.

- Significance earns the right to ask the question. Innovation justifies the bet. Approach delivers the work.

- 60% of reviewer comments land in Approach; that is where the most space and the most rigor lives.

elements.content: ## Approach -- Every Aim Has the Same 6-Block Anatomy

elements.alt: A 2x3 grid of Approach sub-blocks for one Aim: Rationale, Methods, Hypothesis, Analyses, Expected outcomes, Problems and alternatives

elements.content: Block 6 (**Problems & Alternatives**) is what separates a competitive Approach from a hopeful one. Reviewers look for it **first**.

slides.speakerNotes:
- 6 blocks per aim. Repeat the structure verbatim for every aim; reviewers expect it.

- Rationale cites paper-cards (Week-5 corpus is the source).

- Methods + Analyses are where preliminary data is referenced.

- Problems & Alternatives is the de-risking block: name what could go wrong, name your fallback. Skipping it reads as naive.

elements.alt: Three cards side by side: Rigor and reproducibility (biological variables, authentication, blinding, open standards), Preliminary data (feasibility, effect size, pipeline, one key figure), and Timeline + milestones with a Gantt chart and milestone diamonds

slides.speakerNotes:
- Three under-loved sub-sections of Approach. The agent insists on all three.

- Rigor & reproducibility: ~1 page in R01. Biological variables (NIH SABV), authentication, blinding, open standards (BIDS / HED for the practicum).

- Preliminary data: 'we can already do this' -- a figure that shows the pipeline runs end-to-end on a smaller subset.

- Timeline: a Gantt with milestones. Reviewers read this for feasibility cross-check.

elements.alt: Five-stage grant pipeline with red and amber dashed boomerang arrows looping from the Review stage back to Aims (critical findings) and Strategy (major findings), and a green in-place box for minor findings

items.text: Plays the **senior reviewer** role with the right calibration for the mechanism.
Scores all 5 criteria on 1-9, plus an integrated impact estimate.
Findings tagged **critical / major / minor** -- entry point for the boomerang.
Reads the PDF visually too: layout, whitespace, figure sizing.
**Convergence in one shot is a red flag**, not a green flag.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- The review skill is separate from the writing skill. Different prompt, deliberately adversarial framing.

- It calibrates to the mechanism: R21 is not held to R01 preliminary-data standards.

- Severity tagging is the entry point: critical -> revise Aims, major -> revise Strategy, minor -> in-place edit. The pipeline icon shows the cycles.

- It will also render the PDF and read each page visually, flagging whitespace + figure-sizing issues. Useful for catching layout problems before submission.

elements.alt: Three panels: A0 summary statement with three reviewer critiques, A1 Introduction page with point-by-point responses, and A1 Strategy with vertical change bars in the margin

elements.content: **Respond, do not rebut.** Mark every change. The Introduction page is 1 page; the body shows the work. The skill drafts the point-by-point response and inserts change bars in the LaTeX source.

slides.speakerNotes:
- A1 resubmissions get a 1-page Introduction page where you address every reviewer concern point-by-point.

- Bold the changes in the body, or use vertical change bars in the margin -- LaTeX `changes` package handles this cleanly.

- Tone is everything. Rebut only the genuinely wrong critiques; otherwise just respond. Reviewers see rebuttals as defensive.

elements.alt: A two-panel figure (boy vs puppy ERSP) on the left with a compliance checklist on the right showing pass items (resolution, fonts, colour-blind safe, axes legible) and fail items (no error bars, caption duplicates body, whitespace waste)

elements.content: Checks **compliance**, not aesthetics: resolution >= 300 dpi, embedded fonts, colour-blind safe palette, axis-label legibility at printed size, whitespace economy. Runs against the rendered PDF, not the source.

slides.speakerNotes:
- grant-figure-qa is a Claude Code agent (under plugins/grant/agents/) that converts each PDF page to PNG and reads them visually.

- It catches things that source-level review cannot: text that fell off the page at print, fonts that did not embed, figures that are colour-blind unsafe.

- 'Whitespace economy' matters: a half page of empty space in your Approach is a missed page of preliminary data or rigor.

elements.alt: Three labelled shields: NOFO contract (Step 0), Cite-the-card (Aims and Strategy), and Review boomerang (grant-review)

elements.content: Stack-redundant by design. A proposal that converges on the first self-review has either nothing to say or is hiding what it found.

slides.speakerNotes:
- This is the moral of the session. Land the punchline.

- NOFO contract guards scope drift at Step 0.

- Cite-the-card guards hallucinated references at Aims and Strategy time.

- Boomerang guards one-shot convergence at review time.

- They are deliberately redundant: none alone is enough, but together they make rigor mechanical.

items.text: **Topic.** R21 Specific Aims for the HBN naturalistic-movie practicum, drawing on the Week-5 corpus.
**Live 1.** `/grant:grant-writing` -- draft the overarching goal and Aim 1.
**Live 2.** `/grant:grant-review` -- walk through whatever findings surface.
**Live 3.** `/grant:grant-figure-qa` on the preliminary-data figure from Week 5.

elements.content: We do **not** manufacture a finding. If one surfaces, we walk the boomerang back to Aim 1 -- described aloud, not run on stage.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 6x to reveal fragments]

- The demo continues the Week-3 / Week-5 practicum thread on naturalistic movies.

- An R21 fits the scope better than an R01: pilot data on 183 children for a high-risk animacy-of-shot effect.

- We will not generate a fake critical finding -- if the self-review flags 'preliminary data does not yet show the effect', that is exactly the kind of honest boomerang we want.

elements.content: ## What Today Gives You / What's Next

items.text: **Today.** A Specific Aims page grounded in the Week-5 corpus, with a 1-page response-ready review.
**Today.** Three defences for grants -- NOFO contract, cite-the-card, boomerang.
**Today.** Same workflow in Claude Code, Codex CLI, or Copilot in VS Code.
**Week 7.** Manuscripts -- Aims become the Introduction; Approach becomes Methods + Results.
**Week 8.** Scientific Figures -- the preliminary-data figure gets a full publication pass.
**Week 9.** Neuroinformatics -- BIDS / HED standards the Rigor section just promised.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 7x to reveal fragments]

- Take-home in two sentences: a grant is a 1-page lit review plus deliverables; the same agent toolkit drafts and reviews it.

- The closing line bridges into the demo and Q&A. Latency is OK; the audience can interject while opencite or grant-writing runs.
